ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) a rotation of a point about the origin through an angle maps it to another point such that p and and p’ are at the same distance from the origin and the angle from the vector p to the vector p’ is to determine the coordinates of the image point , it is very convenient to use polar coordinates.

item.title: affine transformation matrix
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) consider the following simple equation: we can define the (infinite) set of vectors that these equations span which can also be written in the usual function form note that this isnt a linear transformation, but an affine transformation, because a transformation matrix can only be deduced for linear maps, we can turn into a linear map if we drop the intercept which is keeping it from preserving the origin: now that we know for sure is a proper linear function we can treat it as such the standard basis of the domain of is we apply to these vectors: we place these in a matrix (as column vectors) to get the transformation matrix for now we bring back the intercept, to transform a given vector using the matrix formula would be: for convenience, we use homogeneous coordinates to turn this formula into just one matrix multiplication by adding one column and one row and placing the intercept in the last column with an additional to denote that its a point (homogenous coordinates notation):

item.title: affine transformation
item.description: unlike a purely linear transformation, an affine transformation need not preserve the origin of the affine space. thus, every linear transformation is affine, but not every affine transformation is linear.

item.title: modus ponens
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) modus ponens is a deductive argument form and rule of inference. it can be summarized as “P implies Q. P is true. Therefore Q must also be true.” the form of a modus ponens argument consists of two premises and a conclusion: if \(P\), then \(Q\) \(P\) therefore, \(Q\)

item.title: axiom
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) an axiom is a statement that is taken to be true, to serve as a premise or starting point for further reasoning and arguments. an axiom can be considered a premise, but not every premise can be considered an axiom, as axioms dont require proof and are part of the definition of a formal system

item.title: deduction theorem
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) in a complete formal system, in order to prove \(\alpha 	o  eta\) we can add \(\alpha\) to the set of assumptions and then prove \( eta\) a special case of the deduction theorem is: \(p \vdash q\) iff \(\vdash (p 	o q)\) when writing a formal proof, we write \(IP\) (atleast in my college) to denote the usage of deduction theorem infer \((\lnot P 	o S) 	o S\) from \(P 	o S\)  egin{align} P 	o S &\quad P_1\ \lnot P 	o S &\quad IP\ \lnot S 	o P &\quad 2,E21\ \lnot S 	o S &\quad 1,3,I13\ \lnot (\lnot S) \lor S &\quad 4,E20\ S \lor S &\quad 5,E9\ S &\quad 5,E5 \end{align}

item.title: formal system
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) a formal system is an abstract structure used for inferring theorems from axiom according to a set of rules of inference which are used for carrying out the inference of theorems from axioms. consider the following formal system \(L_	o\): it has the following axioms: A1. \(\alpha 	o ( eta 	o \alpha)\) A2. \((\alpha 	o ( eta 	o \gamma)) 	o ((\alpha 	o  eta) 	o (\alpha 	o \gamma))\) A3.

item.title: logic homework 5
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) homework on formal proofs, using logic formulas, deduction theorem prove/disprove: premises: \(\lnot p \lor q\) \(\lnot q \lor (r \land s)\) \(\lnot r \lor p\) conclusion: \(\lnot (p \lor r)\)  egin{align} (\lnot q \lor r) \land (\lnot q \lor s) &\quad P2,E14\ \lnot q \lor r &\quad 1,I3\ \lnot q \lor s &\quad 1,I4 \end{align}

item.title: common lisp graphics
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) we need the libraries sdl2, livesupport (to be able to continue if an error occurs in the main loop) and opengl for rendering using quicklisp (ql:quickload :sdl2) (ql:quickload :livesupport) (ql:quickload :cl-opengl) here im implementing an abstraction layer over opengl/sdl2 to be able to render things like math functions easily we need a generic way to represent objects that are renderable onto a window so we use the generic function render and implement it in the various classes (defgeneric render (obj) (:documentation "a function to render the object in an opengl context")) (defstruct rgb "rgb color" (r 0) (g 0) (b 0)) (defstruct point-2d () x y (color (make-rgb :r 0 :b 0 :g 0))) (defmethod render ((p point-2d)) (gl:with-primitives :points (gl:vertex (point-2d-x p) (point-2d-y p)))) (defstruct line-2d point1 point2 (color (make-rgb :r 0 :b 0 :g 0)) (width 1)) (defmethod render ((line line-2d)) (let ((point1 (line-2d-point1 line)) (point2 (line-2d-point2 line)) (width (line-2d-width line))) (gl:line-width width) (gl:with-primitives :lines (gl:vertex (point-2d-x point1) (point-2d-y point1)) (gl:vertex (point-2d-x point2) (point-2d-y point2))))) (defclass window () ((draw-axis :initarg :draw-axis :accessor window-draw-axis) (renderables :initform '() :accessor window-renderables) (width :initarg :width :initform 750 :accessor window-width) (height :initarg :height :initform 750 :accessor window-height))) (defmethod window-run ((my-window window)) (sdl2:with-init (:everything) (format t "Using SDL Library Version: ~D.

item.title: argument
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(

ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) an argument is a statement or group of statements called premises intended to determine the degree of truth or acceptability of another statement called a conclusion.

item.title: conclusion
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) a conclusion is a proposition that is reached from the given set of premises. we can think of a conclusion as the result of the assumptions that we made in an argument

item.title: premise
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) a premise is a proposition on the basis of which we would be able to draw a conclusion, we can think of a premise as an evidence or assumption. although n generally a premise is assumed to be true (although it doesnt have to be a tautology, as in it doesnt have to be universally true), as an argument is meaningful for its conclusion only when all of its premises are true.

item.title: rule of inference
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_rules_of_inference a rule of inference is a logical form consisting of a function which takes premises, analyzes their syntax, and returns a conclusion (or conclusions). rules of inference are usually given in the following standard form: \[ \infer{conclusion}{premise_1 & premise_2 & \cdots & premise_n} \] a rule of inference is sound if the conclusion logically follows from the premises the following isnt a sound rule of inference \[ \infer{ eta}{\alpha \lor  eta} \] the following is a sound rule of inference \[ \infer{ eta}{\alpha \land  eta} \] we arrive from premises to a conclusion with the inference process

item.title: formal proof
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) a formal proof is a finite sequence of premises each of which is an axiom, an assumption, or follows from the preceding statements in the sequence by a rule of inference, which are used to prove arguments. a proof sequence from \(\Gamma\) to a proposition \(a\) is one such that \(a\) is the last proposition in the sequence which we call the conclusion.

item.title: logical implication
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) a proposition \(\alpha\) logically implies the proposition \( eta\) if in every model in which \(a\) is true, \( eta\) is also true. we write \(\alpha \implies  eta\) \((\alpha \implies  eta) \iff (\alpha 	o  eta 	ext{ is a tautology})\) a set of propositions \(\Gamma\) logically implies the proposition \( eta\) if in every model in which all propositions of \(\Gamma\) are correct \( eta\) is also correct.

item.title: truth table
item.description: a truth table maps variables to their corresponding outputs when passed into a boolean expression or a switching function

item.title: equivalence
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) statements \(p\) and \(q\) are said to be logically equivalent if they have the same truth value in every model. proving equivalence can be done: using a truth table and verifying that all rows are similar using already proven equivalences using an inference process proving \(\lnot (A \lor B) \land B \lor D \equiv D\)  egin{align*} \lnot (A \lor B) \land B \lor D &\equiv (\lnot A \land \lnot B) \land B \lor D & E16\ &\equiv \lnot A \land (\lnot B \land B) \lor D & E13\ &\equiv \lnot A \land F \lor D & E8\ &\equiv (\lnot A \land F) \lor D\ &\equiv F \lor D & E4\ &\equiv D & E2 \end{align*}

item.title: logic formulas
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) note that \(T\) and \(F\) represent true and false, respectively 1 equivalences number equivalence rule name E1 \(A \lor T \equiv T\) E2 \(A \lor F \equiv A\) E3 \(A \land T \equiv A\) E4 \(A \land F \equiv F\) E5 \(A \lor A \equiv A\) E6 \(A \land A \equiv A\) E7 \(A \lor \lnot A \equiv T\) E8 \(A \land \lnot A \equiv F\) E9 \(\lnot (\lnot A) \equiv A\) E10 \(A \lor B \equiv B \lor A\) E11 \(A \land B \equiv B \land A\) E12 \((A \lor B) \lor D \equiv A \lor (B \lor D)\) E13 \((A \land B) \land D \equiv A \land (B \land D)\) E14 \(A \lor (B \land D) \equiv (A \lor B) \land (A \lor D)\) E15 \(A \land (B \lor D) \equiv (A \land B) \lor (A \land D)\) E16 \(\lnot (A \lor B) \equiv \lnot A \land \lnot B\) de morgan E17 \(\lnot (A \land B) \equiv \lnot A \lor \lnot B\) de morgan E18 \(A \lor (A \land B) \equiv A\) E19 \(A \land (A \lor B) \equiv A\) E20 \(A 	o B \equiv \lnot A \lor B\) E21 \(A 	o B \equiv (\lnot B) 	o (\lnot A)\) E22 \((A \leftrightarrow B) \equiv (A 	o B) \land (B 	o A)\) E23 \(A 	o (B 	o C) \equiv (A \land B) 	o C\) 2 logical implication number implication rule name I1 \(R \implies R \lor S\) I2 \(S \implies R \lor S\) I3 \(R \land S \implies R\) I4 \(R \land S \implies S\) I5 \(\{R,S\} \implies R \land S\) I6 \(\{\lnot R, (R \lor S)\} \implies S\) I7 \(S \implies (R 	o S)\) I8 \(\lnot R \implies (R 	o S)\) I9 \(\lnot (R 	o S) \implies R\) I10 \(\lnot (R 	o S) \implies \lnot S\) I11 \(\{R,(R 	o S)\} \implies S\) I12 \(\{\lnot S, (R 	o S)\} \implies \lnot R\) I13 \(\{(P 	o R),(R 	o S)\} \implies (P 	o S)\) I14 \(\{(P \lor R),(P 	o S),(R 	o S)\} \implies S\)

item.title: decision tree
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) <&aima2010> a decision tree represents a function that takes as input a vector of attribute values and returns a “decision”–a single output value. the input and output values can be discrete or continuous for a wide variety of problems, the decision tree yields a nice, concise result. but some functions cannot be represented concisely, for example, the majority function, which returns true if and only if more than half of the inputs are true, requires an exponentially large decision tree.

item.title: feedforward neural network
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) <&aima2010> a feedforward network has connections only in one direction. each node computes a function of its inputs and passes the result to its successors in the network. information flows through the network from the input nodes to the output nodes, and there are no loops. a unit calculates the weighted sum of the inputs from predecessor nodes and then applies a nonlinear function to produce its output.

item.title: sbcl i/o memory leak
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) i was trying to load the mnist dataset into sbcl when i found this frustrating memory leak (defun get-file (filename) (with-open-file (stream filename) (loop for line = (read-line stream nil) while line collect line))) (loop for j from 0 below 20 do (defparameter a (get-file (pathname "/home/mahmooz/brain/notes/data/2c/cba5d6-f153-454d-9e49-004b56167fbb/mnist_train.csv")))) i ran sbcl with 1gb heap size using sbcl --dynamic-space-size 1GB sbcl would crash with the following error: * Heap exhausted during garbage collection: 3696 bytes available, 7312 requested.

item.title: training on mnist
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) got the mnist in csv format from https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/oddrationale/mnist-in-csv im gonna use a feedforward neural network: first we load the dataset using the cl-csv library (ql:quickload "cl-csv") (defparameter *train-data* (cl-csv:read-csv (pathname train-data-file))) (defparameter *test-data* (cl-csv:read-csv (pathname test-data-file))) (defparameter *train-data* (mapcar (lambda (mylist) (mapcar #'parse-integer mylist)) (cdr *train-data*))) (defparameter *test-data* (mapcar (lambda (mylist) (mapcar #'parse-integer mylist)) (cdr *test-data*))) next we create the network, each image is 28x28 pixels so we need an input layer of that size, and an output layer of size 10 because it needs to output 0-9 (defparameter nw (make-network :input-layer-size (* 28 28) :hidden-layer-size 10 :hidden-layers-number 1 :learning-rate 0.

ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(

ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) homework on functionally complete set of connectives given the functions  egin{gather*} h(x,y,z) = \lnot x \land (\lnot y 	o z)\ g(x,y,z) = x 	o (y 	o z) \end{gather*} is \(\{h\}\) a functionally complete set of connectives? \(h(x,y,z) = \lnot x \land (\lnot y 	o z) = \lnot x \land (y \lor z)\) i think we can replicate F \(h(x,x,x)=\lnot x \land (x \lor x) = \lnot x \land x = F\) we cant replicate negation and conjunction (or disjunction) is \(\{g\}\) a functionally complete set of connectives?

ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) we need to improve 2 things to make this algorithm run faster first we need to have an efficient way to lookup the minimal edge in \(E_{rem}\), this can be done by sorting the edges of the graph in an ascending order of weights second, we need an efficient method to check whether an edge connects two trees, this is complicated, to solve this we introduce the union/find algorithm then we reimplement kruskal’s algorithm with the help of \(	extsc{Union/Find}\)

item.title: minimum spanning tree
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) \(
ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) given an undirected, connected and weighted graph \(G=(V,E,w)\). let \(T=(V,E’)\) be a spanning tree of \(G\). the weight of \(T\) is the sum of weights of its edges. i.e. \[ W(T) = \sum_{e \in E’} w(e) \] the tree \(T\) is a minimum spanning tree if for every other spanning tree \(T_1\) of \(G\), the following is true: \[ W(T_1) \geq W(T) \] a graph may have multiple mst’s let \(T\) be an mst of \(G\) and let \(e \in T\).

ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) <&aima2010> we can easily extend from univariate linear regression to multivariate linear regression, such that each example \(x_j\) is an n-element vector. our hypothesis space is the set of functions of the form \[ h_{ rm{w}}( rm{x}_j) = w_0+w_1x_{j,1} + \dots + w_nx_{j,n} = w_0+\sum_{i} w_ix_{j,i} \] the \(w_0\) term, the intercept, stands out as different from the others.

ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) cross-entropy, written as \(H(P,Q)\), is a kind of measure of dissimilarity between two distributions \(P\) and \(Q\). the general definition is \[ H(P,Q) =  rm{E}_{ rm{z} \sim P( rm{Z})}j[\log Q( rm{z})] = \int P( rm{z})\log Q( rm{z}) \dif{ rm{z}} \]

ewcommand rm[1]{	extbf{#1}}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}\ \(\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmin}{arg\,min}\) write the following expression in DNF and CNF forms \((p ightarrow (q ightarrow r)) ightarrow ((p ightarrow q) ightarrow (p ightarrow r))\) in dnf form:  egin{align*} (p ightarrow (q ightarrow r)) ightarrow ((p ightarrow q) ightarrow (p ightarrow r)) &\equiv \lnot(\lnot p \lor (\lnot q \lor r)) \lor (\lnot(\lnot p \lor q) \lor (\lnot p \lor r))\ &\equiv \lnot(\lnot p \lor \lnot q \lor r) \lor \lnot(\lnot p \lor q) \lor (\lnot p \lor r)\ &\equiv \lnot(\lnot p \lor \lnot q \lor r) \lor \lnot(\lnot p \lor q) \lor \lnot p \lor r\ &\equiv \lnot(\lnot p \lor \lnot q \lor r) \lor (p \land \lnot q) \lor \lnot p \lor r\ &\equiv (p \land q \land \lnot r) \lor (p \land \lnot q) \lor \lnot p \lor r\ \end{align*}

item.title: weighted graph
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a weighted graph is \(G=(V,E,w)\) such that \(G=(V,E)\) is a graph (directed or not) and \(w: E 	o R\) is a weight function that gives each edge \(e \in E\) a value \(w(e)\). this value is called the weight or length of the edge \(e\) let \(G=(V,E,w)\) be a weighted graph, let \(\alpha=u_0,u_1,\dots,u_l\) be a path in \(G\), the length of the path \(\alpha\) is defined as the sum of weights of the edges in the path \[ α =∑i=0l-1 w(u_i,ui+1) \]

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) Huffman code is a particular type of optimal prefix code that is commonly used for lossless data compression the problem that huffman code solves is the following: input: a leaf array that corresponds to a file \(F\) output: optimal code tree of \(F\), \(T_{out}\) consider the following leaf array char freq a 41 b 17 c 14 d 12 e 9 f 7 this is the application of huffman coding to the leaf array so we end up with the following code tree: the process begins with the leaf nodes containing the frequencies of the symbol they represent.

item.title: graph
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a graph is an ordered pair \(G = (V, E)\), where \(V\) is a set whose elements are called vertices, and \(E\) is a set of paired vertices, whose elements are called edges consider the graph \(G_1=(V_1,E_1),V_1=\{a,b,c\},E_1=\{(a,b),(a,c),(a,a),e_1=(b,c),e_2=(b,c)\) consider the following algorithm which receives a non-directed graph and returns a list of its edges the time complexity of this algorithm is \(O(|E|+|V|)\) the maximum number of edges in a simple non-directed graph is \[  rac{|V| \cdot (|V|-1)}{2} \] in a complete graph, the rank of each vertex is \(|V|-1\), and it follows trivially that the sum of ranks is \(|V|(|V|-1)\), and since each vertex touches 2 edges, the number of edges is \(|E|= rac{|V| \cdot (|V|-1)}{2}\) let \(G=(V,E)\) be a graph, a path from the a \(v_0\) to another \(v_1\) is a sequence of edges \[ \alpha = (v_0,v_1),(v_1,v_2),\dots,(v_{l-1},v_l) \] such that the first edge is between \(v_0\) and \(v_1\), the second is between \(v_1\) and \(v_2\), and in general, for all \(i\), \(1 \le i \le l\) the \(i\)’th edge is between \(v_{i-1}\) and \(v_i\).

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) <&aima2010> entropy is a measure of uncertainty of a random variable a random variable with only one value–a coin that always comes up heads–has no uncertainty and thus its entropy is defined as zero; thus, we gain no information by observing its value. a flip of a fair coin is equally likely to come up heads or tails, 0 or 1, this counts as “1 bit” of entropy.

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) implementation of trees in common lisp see trees in lisp (defun tree-to-latex-forest-for-org (tree) "turn a tree into a runnable org mode latex block" (format t "#+begin_src latex :results file graphics :file (temp-file \"svg\") ~%\begin{forest}") (tree-to-latex-forest-recurse tree) (format t "\end{forest}~%#+end_src")) (defun tree-to-latex-forest-recurse (tree) "helper function for tree-to-latex-forest-for-org" (if (not (eq tree nil)) (progn (format t "[~a,circle,draw" (car tree)) (loop for child in (cdr tree) do (tree-to-latex-forest-recurse child)) (format t "]")) (format t "[,phantom]")) tree) (let ((my-tree '(23 (12) (12 (1 nil (4 nil (5 nil (9 nil (10 nil nil))))) nil) (28 nil (45 nil (89 nil nil)))))) (tree-to-latex-forest-for-org my-tree))

item.title: select algorithm
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) an algorithm that would help us reduce the time complexity of many problems input: an array \(A\) of \(n\) numbers and a normal number \(k\) such that \(1 \le k \le n\) output: the element in \(A\) whose rank is \(k\), i.e. the \(k\)’th smallest number in \(A\) example: for the input \(k=3,A=\{13,15,16,14,12,18,17,11\}\), the output is 13 a naive approach: the correctness of this algorithm trivially follows from the definition of the given problem we propose a more afficient algorithm that we’ll call \(	extsc{Select}\) which solves the problem in linear time: given an array \(A\) of \(n\) different numbers, and a number \(k\), \(1 \le k \le n\), let \(e_k\) be a number such that \(ank(e_k,A)=k\) assume that the partition algorithm was applied to \(A\) and we got the subarrays \(A_l,A_r\) of sizes \(m,n-m\) if \(k \le m\) then \(e_k\) is in \(A_l\) and \(ank(e_k,A_l)=k\) if \(k>m\) then \(e_k\) is in \(A_r\) and \(ank(e_k,A_r)=k-m\) for example, given the array \(A=\{3,8,-2,13,11,6,9,2,14,7\},pivot=8,k=6\) we’d get \(A_l=\{3,-2,6,2,7\},A_r=\{8,13,11,9,14\}\) in \(A_l\) there are the 5 smallest numbers from \(A\), the sixth smallest number in A, 8, is the minimal number in \(A_r\) the correctness of the algorithm follows trivially from the lemma we are sacrificing linear time finding a good pivot so that the partition algorithm’s worst-case performance doesnt reach \(\Theta(n^2)\), which may happen when the size of one of the partitions happens to be 1 repeatedly this algorithm runs in linear time a company has \(n\) employees, their salaries differ and are given in an array \(A\), it wants to give its employees bonuses (it wants to spend as much as it can, given a limited budget), a bonus is the same amount as ones salary, given \(k\), the sum of bonuses is less or equal to a given number \(k\), solve the problem in \(\Theta(n)\) the recurrence formula for this algorithm is \(T(n)=Cn+T\left( rac{n}{2}ight)\) which simplifies to \(T(n) \in \Theta(n)\)

item.title: recursion tree
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) consider the following \[ T(n) = T\left( rac{n}{3}ight) + T\left( rac{2n}{3}ight) + n \] a note to keep in mind is that the sum of all the nodes of the tree must always be equal to \(T(n)\) with that in mind, the first step would be: if we sum all the nodes we can see that indeed \(T(n) = T\left(\dfrac{2n}{3}ight) + T\left(\dfrac{n}{3}ight) + n\) for the next step we need to write \(T\left(\dfrac{2n}{3}ight)\) and \(T\left(\dfrac{n}{3}ight)\) in terms of time complexity for smaller values of \(n\) so we can know what the next row of nodes would be  egin{align*} T\left( rac{2n}{3}ight) &= T\left( rac{2 \cdot  rac{2n}{3}}{3}ight) + T\left( rac{ rac{2n}{3}}{3}ight) +  rac{2n}{3} = T\left( rac{4n}{9}ight) + T\left( rac{2n}{9}ight) +  rac{2n}{3}\ T\left( rac{n}{3}ight) &= T\left( rac{2 \cdot  rac{n}{3}}{3}ight) + T\left( rac{ rac{n}{3}}{3}ight) +  rac{n}{3} = T\left( rac{2n}{9}ight) + T\left( rac{n}{9}ight) +  rac{n}{3} \end{align*}

item.description:

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) to solve a , we find a closed form for it, a closed form for \(T(n)\) is an equation that defines \(T(n)\) using an expression that does not involve \(T\) there is no single method that works for all, we check for patterns and use substitution to get a better understanding, we look at examples consider the following function that returns the factorial of a given number def fac(n): if n == 1: return 1 else: return n * fac(n-1) print([fac(i) for i in range(1,10)]) for fac(n), how many times is fac called?

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) to analyze the time complexity of recursive functions we use a mathematical concept called recurrence relation a recurrence defines \(T(n)\) in terms of \(T\) for smaller values \(T(n) = T(n-1) + 1\), here \(T(n)\) is defined in terms of \(T(n-1)\)

item.title: algorithm correctness
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) an algorithm is totally correct if it receives valid input, terminates, and always returns the correct output the only way to prove the correctness of an algorithm over all possible inputs is by reasoning formally or mathematically about it. generally, to prove an algorithm is correct we prove two things the algorithm terminates the algorithm returns the correct value for every input we propose a statement that describes what the algorithm takes as input and what its supposed to output and then prove it 1 proving algorithm correctness by induction here we’ll be using mathematical induction for each input that is an array of numbers A, the algorithm \(	extsc{Sum-Array}\) computes the sum of the numbers proof the algorithm terminates: the algorithm consists of a for loop that is bound by n, so it follows trivially that it must terminate proof of correctness by perfect induction on the length of the input array we propose the following loop invariant for the loop \[ sum = \sum_{j=1}^{i} A[j] \] in this case, proving the proposed loop invariant would prove the algorithm correct for \(i=1\), which is the base case, the algorithm returns A[1] which would be the only element in the array, so its obvious that its the correct output the induction hypothesis, we assume after i iterations in the loop that \(sum = \sum_{j=1}^{i} A[j]\) the induction step, during the \(i+1\)’th iteration of the loop, we add \(A[i+1]\) to the variable sum, and so it follows from this and from the induction hypothesis that: \[ sum = \sum_{j=1}^{i} A[j] + A[i+1] = \sum_{j=1}^{i+1} A[j] \] the loop runs n times, by substituting n in the loop invariant, we’d get \[ sum = \sum_{j=1}^{n} A[j] \] which means that after the loop is done, the variable sum would contain the sum of the elements in the array, which is what the function returns, which is the correct output QED 2 correctness of recursive algorithms the pattern for proofs of recursive algorithms is as follows: base step of the recursion, we have to manually show that for each of the edge cases the output is correct induction hypothesis, we assume that in each recurring call the output is correct proof, we show that the output is correct for the main function call consider the following function proof that the algorithm terminates: assume that \(	extsc{Recursive-Sum}\) runs on an array A, on each recurring call, the size of the input array A is halved, its obvious that at the end of every sequence of calls the size of the input array would arrive to 1 which is the edge case that would cause the function to terminate proof of correctness: we prove this by induction on the number of elements in the array base case: for \(|A|=1\) which is the edge case, the algorithm returns \(A[1]\), the only element in \(A\), its obvious that the output is correct induction hypothesis: we use perfect induction on the size of the input, we assume that the algorithm correctly computes the sum of the elements of any array whose size is smaller than n induction step: let A be an arbitrary array of size n, the call to \(	extsc{Recursive-Sum}(A)\) executes the calls \(	extsc{Recursive-Sum}(A_l)\) and \(	extsc{Recursive-Sum}(A_r)\), the size of the arrays \(A_l\) and \(A_r\) is smaller than \(n\), so according to the previous step we know those two recurring calls do return the correct sum of the corresponding subarrays, and since we are returning the sum of those sums we know for sure we are returning the correct sum

item.title: algorithms homework 3
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) homework on recursion please do provide feedback on mistakes you find, if any write a recursive algorithm that finds the 2 biggest numbers in a given array that halves the array on each recursive call python version of the algorithm: def biggest_two(arr): if len(arr) == 1: return arr[0], -1000 if len(arr) == 2: return arr[0], arr[1] left_1, left_2 = biggest_two(arr[0:len(arr)//2]) right_1, right_2 = biggest_two(arr[len(arr)//2:len(arr)]) biggest_1 = max({left_1, left_2, right_1, right_2}) biggest_2 = max({left_1, left_2, right_1, right_2} - {biggest_1}) return biggest_1, biggest_2 print(biggest_two([10, 20, 30, 35, 2, 17])) (35, 30) find the time complexity of the algorithm we consider the \(T(n)=2T\left( rac{n}{2}ight) + C\):  egin{align*} T(n) &= 2T\left( rac{n}{2}ight) + C\ T\left( rac{n}{2}ight) &= 2T\left( rac{n}{4}ight) + C\ T\left( rac{n}{4}ight) &= 2T\left( rac{n}{8}ight) + C\ T\left( rac{n}{2^{\lg n}}ight) &= 2T\left( rac{n}{2^{\lg n+1}}ight) + C\ T(n) &= 2\left(2T\left( rac{n}{4}ight) + Cight) + C = 4T\left( rac{n}{4}ight) + 3C\ &= 4\left(2T\left( rac{n}{8}ight)+Cight)+3C = 8T\left( rac{n}{8}ight)+7C\ &= 8\left(2T\left( rac{n}{16}ight)+Cight)+7C = 16T\left( rac{n}{16}ight)+15C\ &\vdots\ &= 2^{\lg n}T\left( rac{n}{2^{\lg n}}ight) + \sum_{i=0}^{\lg n} 2^{i}C\ &= 2^{\lg n}C_1 + \sum_{i=0}^{\lg n} 2^{i}C\ &= 2^{\lg n}C_1 +  rac{1-2^{\lg(n)+1}}{-1}C\ &= 2^{\lg n}C_1-C+2^{\lg(n)+1}\ &\Rightarrow \Theta\left(2^{\lg n}ight) = \Theta(n) \end{align*}

item.title: comparison sort
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a comparison sort is a type of sorting algorithm that only reads the list elements through a single abstract comparison operation (often a “less than or equal to” operator or a three-way comparison) that determines which of two elements should occur first in the final sorted list. any comparison-sort based algorithm has a time complexity that is bound by \(\Omega(n\log n)\) a vector A of size n is defined to be a “hill” iff for every \(i \le i < j \le m\) we know \(A[i] \le A[j]\) and for every \(m \le i < j \le n\) we know \(A[i] \geq A[j]\) where \(m\) is a constant and \(i,j\) are variable indicies prove that any comparison-based algorithm that turns a vector into a hill has to perform \(\Omega(n\log n)\) operations we assume in contradiction that there exists a comparison-based algorithm that turns a vector into a hill whose time complexity is less than \(\Omega(n\log n)\) consider the following function: each line from 3 to 6 runs in \(\Theta(n)\) time, except the first which we know by the assumption runs in less than \(\Theta(n\log n)\), so the whole function runs in less than \(\Theta(n\log n)\) time this is a comparison-based function that sorts a given array in less than \(\Theta(n\log n)\) time, which contradicts with our theorem that no sorting function can have a lower time complexity than \(\Theta(n\log n)\) we arrived at a contradiction and so it has to be true that this function is bound from below by \(\Theta(n\log n)\), i.

item.title: heap sort
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) heap sort consists of transforming the given array into a heap array A, initiating heap-size to size(A) then applying the following steps recursively: replace the root of the heap A[0] with A[heap-size - 1] bubble down the newly replaced root (A[0]) decrement heap-size, return to step 1 if heap-size > 1 the time complexity of this algorithm is \(\Theta(n\log n)\) on the third step when decrementing heap-size we arent deleting the last element, we are simply decreasing the size of the part of the list that we’re operating on because the part at the end contains elements that are sorted

item.title: insertion sort
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) iterate from arr[1] to arr[n] compare the current element to its predecessor if the current element is smaller than its predecessor, drag it backwards until its bigger than its predecessor time complexity is \(O(n^2)\) template <typename T> void insertion_sort(T arr[], int len) { for (int i = 1; i < len; ++i) { int idx = i; while (idx > 0 && arr[idx] < arr[idx-1]) { T tmp = arr[idx]; arr[idx] = arr[idx-1]; arr[idx-1] = tmp; idx--; } } }

\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) QuickSort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm, it picks an element as pivot and partitions the given array around the pivot, there are different versions of quicksort that pick pivots in different ways: first element as pivot (implemented here) last element as pivot a random element as pivot median as pivot the first subarray we use as a “partition” is the array itself, where given a pivot, put the pivot at its correct position in the sorted array by putting all the smaller elements before it, and all the greater elements after it.

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) this algorithm repeatedly steps through the input list element by element, comparing the current element with the one after it, swapping their values if needed. these passes through the list are repeated until no swaps had to be performed during a pass, meaning that the list has become fully sorted. the algorithm, which is a comparison sort, is named for the way the larger elements “bubble” up to the top of the list.

item.title: greedy algorithm
item.description: the method of greediness proposes that in each step in an algorithm, where we are presented with multiple choices, the choice with the biggest instantaneous gain is picked using this method we can write algorithm that, while relatively simple, arent usually optimal input: an array A of positive numbers output: a subarray (non-consecutive) whose sum is maximal which doesnt contain two consecutive elements the algorithm \(\textsc{Greedy1}(A)\) picks the biggest number in the input array, adds it to the total sum, and discards the two neighboring elements but is this algorithm correct?

item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

item.title: algorithms sample exam 1
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) about select algorithm present the computational problem that the algorithm solves show the correctness of the algorithm given the following algorithm that receives as input two arrays of characters, the array \(A\) which contains \(n\) elements and the array \(P\) that contains \(m\) elements, such that \(m<n\) show the computational problem that the algorithm solves i have no fricking idea, \(P[0]\) is getting dereferenced anyway which is an error given an array \(A\) whose elements are integers and whose dimensions are \(n 	imes n\), you have to propose an algorithm that computes the sequence of number in the array whose sum is maximal, the sequence starts with \(A[1,1]\) and ends with \(A[n,n]\), every element in the sequence is one to the right or one below the previous element we use an array \(B\) in which \(B[i,j]\) is defined as the maximal sum under the constraint that \(B[i,j]\) is in the solution the time complexity of algorithm is \(\Theta(n)\)

item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) events A and B are disjoint if they cannot occur at the same time, \(P(A \cap B) = 0\) given A,B are disjoint events, \[ P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) \], and vice versa generally, \(P(A \cup B) = P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B)\) so \(P(A \cap B)=\varnothing\) iff \(P(A \cup B) = P(A)+P(B)\)

item.description:

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a probability distribution the probability for a success on the \(k\)’th attempt with success probability \(p\) is \[ P(k) = {(1-p)}^{k-1}p \] because we have \(k-1\) failures which gives us \({(1-p)}^{k-1}\), and a single success on the last attempt so we multiply by \(p\) a random variable has geometric distributivity if it receives the values \(1,2,\dots\) with probabilities \(P(X=k)\) the symbol of the geometric distribution is \(X \sim G(p)\) the expected number of trials for a success is \(E[k] =  rac{1}{p}\) variance is \(\mathrm{Var}= rac{1-p}{p^2}\) formula of “at most” in a geometric distribution is \(P(x \le k)=1-(1-p)^k\) formula of “at least” \(P(x \geq k)=(1-p)^{k-1}\)

item.title: variance
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) let \(X\) be a random variable whose expected value is finite \(\mu=\mathrm{E}(X)\), the variance of \(X\), denoted by \(\mathrm{Var}(X)\), is defined as \[ \mathrm{Var}(X) = \mathrm{E}\left(\left(X-\muight)^2ight) \] let \(X\) be a random variable whose distribution law is \[ x =  egin{cases} -1 & 1/4\ 0 & 1/4\ 1 & 1/2 \end{cases} \] we find the variance:  egin{gather*} \mu = \mathrm{E}(X) = -1 \cdot  rac{1}{4} + 0 \cdot  rac{1}{4} + 1 \cdot  rac{1}{2} =  rac{1}{4}\ \mathrm{Var}(X) = \mathrm{E}\left(\left(X- rac{1}{4}ight)^2ight) = \left(-1- rac{1}{4}ight)^2 \cdot  rac{1}{4} + \left(0- rac{1}{4}ight)^2 \cdot  rac{1}{4} + \left(1- rac{1}{4}ight) \cdot  rac{1}{2} =  rac{11}{16} \end{gather*}

item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a probability distribution out of \(n\) different items of which \(m\) are of a specific type and \(n-m\) are of another type, we pick \(k\) items without repetition, the probability that from \(k\) items \(r\) are of the first type and \(k-r\) are of the second type is: \[ P_n(k) =  rac{ inom{m}{r}  inom{n-m}{k-r}}{ inom{n}{k}} \] the symbol of the hypergeometric distribution is \(X \sim H(k,m,n)\) expected value is \(\mathrm{E}(x)=n \cdot  rac{R}{N}\) variance is \(\mathrm{Var}(x)= rac{k \cdot m \cdot (n-m) \cdot (n-k)}{n^2 \cdot (n-1)}\)

item.title: binomial distribution
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a probability distribution the probability of a specific incident to occur \(k\) times out of \(n\) times is a combination, so: \[ P(k) =  inom{n}{k}p^k{(1-p)}^{n-k} \] such that \(k=0,1,\dots,n\), \(0 \le P \le 1\) a random variable is binomially distributive with the parameters \(n,p\) and is written as \(X \sim B(n,p)\) if \(X\) receives the values \(0,1,\dots,n\) with the probabilities \(P(X=k)\) (the formula above) the symbol of the binomial distribution is \(X \sim B(n,p)\) expected value is \(E(x)=np\) variance is \(np(1-p)\) 1 bernoulli trial Independent repeated trials of an experiment with exactly two possible outcomes are called Bernoulli trials.

item.title: discrete random variable
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(

ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a random variable is a discrete random variable iff it receives a finite or infinite series \(X_1,X_2,\dots\) of the values of the function \(P_x\) that is defined by \(P_x(X_i)=P(X=X_i)\) where \(i=1,2,\dots\) which is called the probability function of the random variable \(X\) \[ 0 \le P_x(X_i) \le 1 \] \[ \sum_{i} P_x(X_i) = 1 \] if \(X\) is a discrete random variable then the list of values that it could receive and their corresponding probabilities are called the distribution law or the probability law of said variable

item.title: random variable
item.description: a random variable X is a function \(X(\Omega)\) from the sample space to the real line (\(\mathbb{R}\)), such that every event in the sample space corresponds to a real number. \(P_i\) is the probability of receiving the value \(X_i\) and \(P(X)\) is the probability function there are two types of random variables discrete random variable continuous random variable we roll a symmetric coin 3 times \[ \Omega = \{(h,h,h),(h,h,t),(h,t,h),(t,h,h),(t,h,t),(h,t,t),(t,t,h),(t,t,t)\} \] we define \(X\) as the number of times that “heads” was received \(X\) is a random variable with 4 possible values, \(X(\Omega)=0,1,2,3\), \(P(X=0)=\frac{1}{8},P(X=1)=\frac{3}{8},P(X=2)=\frac{3}{8},P(X=3)=\frac{1}{8}\) let \(f_i\) be the frequency of the value \(x_i\), we get \(\sum_{i}^{k} f_i = n\), so \(\overline{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_if_i}{n}\) let \(p_i=\frac{f_i}{n}\) be the probability to get the value \(x_i\), so \(\sum_{i}^{k} p_i=1\), we get \(\overline{x}=\frac{\sum_{1}^{k} x_if_i}{n}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_if_i=\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i \cdot \frac{f_i}{n}=\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_ip_i\) \[ \overline{x} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_ip_i \] let \(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_k\) be arbitrary values and \(f_1,f_2,\dots,f_k\) their frequencies the variances of \(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_k\) is the measure of their scattering around the average \[ S^2 = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (x_i-\overline{x})^2 f_i \] we build off from the previous lemmas: \begin{align*} S^2 &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (x_i-\overline{x})^2 f_i\\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} (x_i^2-2x_i\overline{x}+\overline{x}^2) \cdot \frac{f_i}{n}\\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} (x_i^2-2x_i\overline{x}+\overline{x}^2) \cdot p_i\\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i^2p_i - 2\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_ip_i\overline{x} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \overline{x}^2p_i\\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i^2p_i - 2\overline{x}\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_ip_i + \overline{x}^2\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i\\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i^2p_i - 2\overline{x}^2 + \overline{x}^2\\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i^2p_i - \overline{x}^2 \end{align*}

item.title: two dimensional random variable
item.description: the pair \((X,Y)\) is a random variable if \(X\) and \(Y\) are defined over the same probability space refer to joint probability distribution let \((X,Y)\) be a two dimensional random variable, the probability function (called marginal distribution) of \(X\) is defined as \[ P(X=x)=\sum_{y} P(X=x,Y=y) \] and of \(Y\) is defined as \[P(Y=y)=\sum_{x} P(X=x,Y=y)\] let \((X,Y)\) be a two dimensional random variable, the conditional probability function of \(X\) for \(Y=y\) is \[ P(X=x\mid Y=y)=\frac{P(X=x,Y=y)}{P(Y=y)} \] and similarly, \[ P(Y=y\mid X=x)=\frac{P(X=x,Y=y)}{P(X=x)} \] we throw a symmetric coin with the values 0 and 1, if we get 0 we throw a die, if we get 1 we throw the same coin again.

item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

item.title: expected value
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) consider a discrete random variable \(X\) that receives the values \(X_1,X_2,\dots\) with the probabilities \(P_1,P_2,\dots\) respectively, the expected value of \(X\), denoted by \(\mathrm{E}(X)\), is defined as \(\mathrm{E}(X)=\sum_{i} X_iP_i\) such that the series is absolutely convergent if \(X\) is a constant random variable, i.e. it receives only one value \(C\) with a probability of 1 then \(\mathrm{E}(X)=C\) if \(a\) is constant, then for each random variable whose expected value is finite \(\mathrm{E}(aX)=a\cdot \mathrm{E}(X)\) let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two random variables defined over the same probability space, then \[ \mathrm{E}(X+Y)=\mathrm{E}(X)+\mathrm{E}(Y) \] let \(X \sim B(n,p)\), we look at \(X\) as the number of successes from a sequence of \(n\) independent trials

item.title: frequency
item.description: the frequency of an event \(i\) is the number \(n_{i}\) of times the observation has occurred/recorded in an experiment or study

item.title: joint distribution table
item.description: in a 2 dimensional table, each cell represents the joint probability of the two variables, i.e. \(P(X=x,Y=y)\) the sum of all the cells in a joint probability table is 1

item.title: joint probability distribution
item.description: given two random variables that are defined on the same probability space, the joint probability distribution is the corresponding probability distribution on all possible pairs of outputs. it is denoted by \(P(X=x,Y=y)\) \[ P(X=x,Y=y) \geq 0 \] \[ \sum_{x} \sum_{y} P(X=x,Y=y)=1 \]

item.title: binary coding
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a binary coding is an injective function that maps each character of a finite set of characters to string of bits, given a character \(c\), and a binary coding \(k\), \(k( c)\) is called the coding of \(c\), and the number of bits in \(k( c)\) is denoted by \(|k( c)|\) consider the following binary coding function for the set \(C=\{a,b,c,d,e,f\}\)

item.title: file
item.description: a file is a finite sequence of characters

item.title: fixed-length code word
item.description: binary coding \(k\) is fixed-length if for every two characters \(c_j,c_i\), \(|k(c_i)|=|k(c_j)|\)

item.title: prefix code
item.description: prefix code is binary code in which no character is a prefix of another in fixed-length code word, the codes are of the same length, so none of the characters can be a prefix of another, so any fixed-length code is by definition prefix code

item.title: variable-length code word
item.description: binary coding that isnt fixed-length code word is variable length

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a recursive function is a function that calls itself consider factorial def fac(n): if n == 1: return 1 else: return fac(n-1) * n 1 base case A recursive function definition has one or more base cases, meaning input(s) for which the function produces a result trivially (without recurring) 2 recurrence relation to analyze the time complexity of recursive functions we use a mathematical concept called recurrence relation a recurrence defines \(T(n)\) in terms of \(T\) for smaller values example: \(T(n) = T(n-1) + 1\), here \(T(n)\) is defined in terms of \(T(n-1)\) 2.

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) for most data structures i havent implemented deletion yet.. also its good to note that most of the code isnt optimized to hell, but it doesnt really matter since its only for education purposes and you should probably be using the data structures provided by the programming language’s standard library 1 data structure a data structure is a collection of data and operations on said data, the data is represented as objects, and each object x has: key(x): a key that is used to identify the object (usually a number that uniquely identifies the object) info(x) or value(x): contains the data needed to store for the object (usually without the key) for example, if x is a person, key(x) could be the person’s ID, value(x) would be information on said person, like age, height, weight etc.

item.title: binary operation
item.description: a binary operation is an operation that invovles two entities

item.title: unary operation
item.description: an unary operation is an operation that, unlike a binary operation, operates on one entity only

item.title: combinatorics
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) combinatorics is the theory of enumeration, where we look at elements of a set as options rule of sum let \(A,B\) be disjoint finite sets, then \(|A \cup B| = |A| + |B|\) in other words, if the set \(A\) had \(n\) elements (\(n\) options) and \(B\) had \(m\) elements (\(m\) options) such that \(A \cap B = \varnothing\) then there exist \(n+m\) total options to pick from \(A \cup B\) if \(A,B\) are finite sets such that \(A \subseteq B\), then \(|B	extbackslash A| = |B|-|A|\) if a nest had 5 red eggs numbered 1-5 and 3 blue eggs numbered 1-3, how many options do we have if we wanted to pick a single egg?

item.title: asymptotic notation
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) notation name formal definition limit definition \(f(n) = O(g(n))\) Big Oh \(\exists k > 0 \ \exists n_0 \  orall n > n_0 : \vert f(n) \vert \leq k \cdot g(n)\) \(\lim_{n	o\infty}  rac{\vert f(n) \vert}{g(n)} = c\), where \(c \geq 0\) is a constant \(f(n) = \Omega(g(n))\) Big Omega \(\exists k > 0 \ \exists n_0 \  orall n > n_0 : \vert f(n) \vert \geq k \cdot g(n)\) \(\lim_{n	o\infty}  rac{g(n)}{f(n)} = c\), where \(c \geq 0\) is a constant \(f(n) = \Theta(g(n))\) Big Theta \(\exists k_1 > 0\ \exists k_2 > 0\ \exists n_0\  orall n > n_0 : k_1 \cdot g(n) \leq \vert f(n) \vert \leq k_2 \cdot g(n)\) \(\lim_{n	o\infty}  rac{f(n)}{g(n)} = c\), where \(c > 0\) is a constant 1 Big O Big O of a function \(f\), denoted by \(O(f)\), is an upper asymptotic bound of \(f\) 2 Big Omega Big Omega of a function \(f\), denoted by \(\Omega(f)\), is a lower asymptotic bound of \(f\) 3 Big Theta Big Theta of a function \(f\), denoted by \(\Theta(f)\), is tight bound of \(f\), so it is both Big Omega and Big O 4 order of growth the order of growth of an algorithm is an approximation of the time required to run a computer program as the input size increases, the order of growth ignores the constant factor needed for fixed operations and focuses instead on the operations that increase proportional to input size an order of growth is a set of functions whose asymptotic growth behavior is considered equivalent.

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a safe has 5 locks person keys for locks X,V person keys for locks Y,V person keys for locks Y,W person keys for locks Z,X person E has keys for locks V,Z whats the minimal number of persons needed to open the safe?  egin{gather*} X=A+D\ Y=B+C\ Z=D+E\ W=C\ V=A+B+E\ F(A,B,C,D,E) = XYZWV = (A+D)(B+C)(D+E)( C)(A+B+E) = (A+D)(A+B+E)(D+E)(B+C) = (A+B+E)(A+D)(D+E)C = (A+B+E)(D+AE)C \end{gather*}

item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) these are my notes on reading the book which covers the science of artificial intelligence extensively 1 solving problems by searching a solution is an action sequence, so search algorithms work by considering various possible action sequences. the possible action sequences starting at the initial state form a search tree with the initial state at the root; the branches are actions and the nodes correspond to states in the state space of the problem an additional data structure could be used to keep track of nodes that have been already visited in the tree to prevent redundant repetitions, demonstrated in the GRAPH-SEARCH algorithm which expands on the TREE-SEARCH algorithm (the new lines are highlighted) 2 machine learning 2.

item.title: discrete/continuous data
item.description: discrete data is a numerical type of data that includes whole, concrete numbers with specific and fixed data values determined by counting. continuous data includes complex numbers and varying data values measured over a particular time interval.

item.title: mechanics
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) <&kleppner1973> 1 true physics vector a vector in physics may differ a bit from that of a math vector a “true” physics vector is independant of the coordinate system we pick while a non-true physics vector may be dependant and its values may change when we change the coordinate system 2 position vector the position of an arbitrary point P at \((x,y,z)\) is written as \[ r = (x,y,z) = x\ihat + y\jhat + z\khat \] the position vector isnt a true physics vector, because it doesnt really have a direction since it just represents a position in space and so the position vector of a specific point in space may differ from one coordinate system to another, as we can pick any coordinate system we want and it wouldnt affect the position of a point 3 displacement vector if we move from the point \(x_1,y_1,z_1\) to some new position \(x_2,y_2,z_2\) then the displacement defines a “true” vector \(S\) with coordinates \[ S = (x_2-x_1,\ y_2-y_1,\ z_2-z_1) \] a displacement vector is a true physics vector 4 instantaneous velocity instantaneous velocity, usually called just velocity is a quantity that tells us how fast an object is moving at a single point in time and space the SI unit for acceleration is metre per second (\( rac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{s}}\)) velocity can also be defined as the derivative of position with respect to time, as it is the limit of the average velocity as the time interval approaches zero: \[ v = \lim_{\Delta t 	o 0}  rac{x(t+\Delta t)-x(t)}{\Delta t} =  rac{dx}{dt} \] using notation due to Gottfried Leibniz, who independently invented calculus.

item.title: polar coordinate system
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a 2d coordinate system points in the \(x,y\) plane are described not by \((x,y)\) coordinates but by \((r,	heta)\) coordinates, where \(r\) is the magnitude and \(	heta\) is the angle between the vector and the \(x\) axis  egin{align*} r &= \sqrt{x^2+y^2}\ 	heta &= \arctan\left( rac{y}{x}ight) \end{align*} the base vectors of this coordinate system are usually denoted by \(hat\) and \(	hetahat\)  egin{align*} hat(	heta) &= \cos	heta\ihat + \sin	heta\jhat\ 	hetahat(	heta) &= -\sin	heta\ihat + \cos	heta\jhat \end{align*}

item.title: coordinate system
item.description: a coordinate system is a system that uses one or more numbers, or coordinates, to uniquely determine the position of the points or other geometric elements on a manifold such as Euclidean space. the order of the coordinates is significant, and they are sometimes identified by their position in an ordered tuple and sometimes by a letter, as in “the x-coordinate”. the coordinates are taken to be real numbers in elementary mathematics, but may be complex numbers or elements of a more abstract system such as a commutative ring.

item.title: cartesian coordinate system
item.description: the familiar three dimensional coordinate system with the \(x,y,z\) axes

item.title: linear algebra 2 sample exam 2
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) define the concept of a linear operator and \(\ker T\) where \(T\) is a linear operator let \(S,T:V 	o V\) be linear operators prove/disprove \(\ker S = \{0\} \implies \ker(T \circ S) = \{0\}\)  egin{gather*} S(\vec{v}) = \vec0 \implies \vec{v} = \vec0\ T(S(\vec{u})) = \vec0\ S(\vec{u}) = \vec0 \implies T(S(\vec{u})) = \vec0\ S(\vec{u}) 
eq \vec0 \centernot\implies T(S(\vec{u})) 
eq \vec0 \end{gather*}

item.title: rational root theorem
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) the rational root theorem states a constraint on rational solutions of a polynomial \(a_{n}x^{n}+a_{n-1}x^{n-1}+\cdots+a_{0} = 0\) with integer coefficients \(a_{i}\in \mathbb {Z}\) and \(a_{0}, a_{n}\neq 0\) the theorem states that each rational solution \(x = p⁄q\), written in lowest terms so that \(p\) and \(q\) are relatively prime, satisfies: \(p\) is an integer factor of the constant term \(a_0\), and \(q\) is an integer factor of the leading coefficient \(a_n\).

item.title: mental health
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) 1 mental illness unlike physical illness, mental illness is related to problems that start in the brain. the brain is an organ. just like any other organs in our body, it can experience changes (healing or injury) based on life experiences like stress, trauma, rejection, lack of sleep, nutrition… although Psychiatry has advanced so much over the years to provide us with the knowledge and tools to deal with mental illness, nothing, not even science or religion can give us a better understanding of mental illness than art, thats why i will mostly focus on art rather than on the study of Psychology or Psychiatry 1.

item.title: probability
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) probability is a function, denoted by \(P\) that describes the probability for a specific incident to occur The probability of an impossible event is zero; the probability of a certain event is one. therefore, for any event \(A\), the range of possible probabilities is \(0 \le P(A) \le 1\) for \(S\) the sample space of all possibilities, \(P(S) = 1\). that is the sum of all the probabilities for all possible events is equal to one.

item.title: symacs
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) symacs, which stands for symbolic emacs is a latex-based interface to sympy built with emacs integration in mind symacs looks for common math writing patterns and parses them into sympy objects to then evaluate them symbolically 1 motivation for a latex-based computer algebra system interface 2 sage vs sympy sagemath is far sure much more powerful than sympy, but sympy is just a python library (about 10mb) and sagemath packs lots of software (including sympy and maxima) that amount to gigabytes of space, so i decided to just go with sympy, which hopefully will be much more powerful in the future as it has a huge community 3 parsing latex math expressions texsoup does all of the work from TexSoup import TexSoup mytex = r'$E_1=\pmqty{1 & 0\\ 0 & 1},E_2=\pmqty{0 & 1\\ 1 & 0},E_3=\pmqty{1 & 0\\ 0 & -1},E_4=\pmqty{0 & 1\\ -1 & 0}$' soup = TexSoup(mytex) print(soup.

item.title: gpg
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) gnupg is an encryption tool 1 encrypt file with password gpg -o <output-file> -c <file-to-encrypt> the argument -o takes the path to the output file (the encrypted one) and the flag -c tells gpg that we want to encrypt, you will be prompted to enter a password 2 decrypt file with password gpg -o <output-file> -d <file-to-decrypt> the argument -o takes the path to the output file (the decrypted one) and the flag -d tells gpg that we want to decrypt 3 encrypt directory with password to encrypt a directory, we simply compress it into a file: tar -cvf archive.
